critic_raw:
The treatment efficacy auxiliary task label is poorly defined and potentially leaky. The design describes the efficacy label as 'resolution of primary diagnosis marker, medication adherence, functional status' within 14 days post-discharge. Medication adherence and functional status at 14 days post-discharge are clinical outcomes that depend on events after the index hospitalization — the very kind of post-discharge information that should be excluded from features to prevent label leakage. If the model learns to predict efficacy as an auxiliary task, and efficacy is correlated with readmission risk (patients who adhere to medication are less likely to be readmitted), then the auxiliary task may encode post-discharge outcome information that would otherwise be forbidden.

Section 7 describes a critical test set contamination: 'a final configuration verification is conducted on the test set to confirm the chosen hyperparameter configuration maintains its performance edge... After this verification, the test set results guide the decision on whether to train the model on both train and validation data.' This is explicit test set-guided model selection and training decisions. Once the test set informs whether to retrain on combined data, the test set can no longer provide an unbiased generalization estimate.

The preprocessing states transformers are 'fit on the combined train and validation set before finalizing the model.' This is a partial improvement over fitting on the full dataset, but it still leaks validation-set statistics into training preprocessing. Preprocessing should be fit on the training set only.

The efficiency definition is operationalized as 'higher discriminative performance (AUROC) with lower model complexity (parameter count, training time).' However, the experiment does not pre-specify a formal criterion for balancing these two dimensions — if the MTL model has higher AUROC but more parameters, is the hypothesis supported? This ambiguity in the success criterion makes post-hoc interpretation susceptible to cherry-picking.
all_issues_raised: Auxiliary task label leakage: treatment efficacy uses post-discharge outcomes (medication adherence, functional status at 14 days) that are correlated with the readmission target
Test set contamination: Section 7 explicitly uses test set performance to guide retraining decisions after validation-based hyperparameter selection
Preprocessing leakage: transformers fitted on combined train+validation set rather than training set only
Underspecified success criterion for 'efficiency': no pre-specified formula for trading off AUROC against parameter count creates post-hoc interpretation flexibility
Missing efficacy label sensitivity analysis is described but ~50,000 encounters with missing efficacy labels is not quantified, and missing label patterns may be informative (MNAR)

Preprocessing leakage via combined train+validation fitting: imputation, scaling, and feature selection are fit on the combined train and validation set, allowing validation-set statistics to influence feature selection and scaling parameters used in final model evaluation

Temporal coherence risk in auxiliary task: post-discharge efficacy labels (derived within 14 days of discharge) overlap with the 30-day readmission window, potentially encoding information about the primary outcome trajectory into the auxiliary training signal

Efficiency metric underspecified: the claim of 'higher AUROC with lower complexity' is not operationalized with pre-specified bounds on complexity increase per unit of performance gain, making the efficiency claim unfalsifiable

TF-IDF dimensionality unspecified: vocabulary size and maximum features for TF-IDF vectorization of clinical notes are unspecified, with uncontrolled high-dimensional sparse features at risk of dominating the feature space

Missing efficacy label availability assessment: the design does not confirm that 14-day post-discharge efficacy labels are available for all patients and how missing labels (patients lost to follow-up within 14 days) are handled beyond a vague 'sensitivity analysis' mention

Test set results explicitly guide the retraining decision ('test set results guide the decision on whether to train the model on both train and validation data') — this is unambiguous test-set contamination that invalidates the reported generalization estimate
Feature selection and preprocessing fit on combined train+validation set, meaning validation-set label information can influence which features are selected and how imputation is performed
Efficacy label is a 14-day composite post-discharge metric; patients readmitted within 14 days or who die early will have label values confounded with the readmission outcome itself

Missing efficacy labels retained with sensitivity analysis rather than principled imputation; if missing labels are not missing at random (e.g., sicker patients have more missing follow-up), this introduces selection bias into the auxiliary task

No specification of statistical significance threshold or effect size for concluding that MTL improves over single-task learning; the efficiency comparison (parameter count, training time) is defined but the performance bar is not
